con_a_pers_elab: Constituion A revolves more around high levels of intelligence, factual accuracy, and fewer hallucinations. I use AI to aid me in my field of machine learning, so high intelligence and accuracy is paramount
con_a_gov_elab: Constituion A also mentions upholding ethical standards, I think when using AI as a governing source the ethical standards begin to matter much more. B does not mention anything about ethics which is why I choose A

con_b_pers_elab: I think B is more thorough. I would trust it more due to the effort put into providing the answer.
con_b_govern_elab: Due to the fact that I want direct answers on this topic, A would be more desirable.

con_a_pers_elab: I like option B because it is more to the point.  The objectives are short and easy to understand.
con_a_gov_elab: I prefer option A for the population.  I like how it addresses that it will not provide false or harmful information to anyone.

con_a_pers_elab: It reads more professionally. It also is more thorough and expands on each point.
con_a_gov_elab: Each topic is explained in more detail. It also makes each topic easier to understand.

con_a_pers_elab: My use of AI is very basic and I prefer a straight forward response over the extra content or engagement that has no impact on my request.
con_a_gov_elab: I prefer a direct and straight forward response that is as condensed and streamlined as possible.

con_b_pers_elab: Constitution A is more concise, simple, and easier to understand.
con_b_govern_elab: Constitution A is more concise, simple, and easier to understand.

con_a_pers_elab: I think that Constitution B consists of standard requirements for conversational AI. However, Constitution A requires conversational AI to go above and beyond, causing the conversational AI to provide excellent responses.
con_a_gov_elab: Constitution A listed higher standards for governing AI behavior.

con_b_pers_elab: I do not want misinformation or simplified answer.
con_b_govern_elab: I think some users would find the language in Constitution B harder to follow.

con_a_pers_elab: I just feel I like the way Constitution A is worded (balanced, friendly, respectful, versatile), while I do like B's almost as much (direct, focused, detailed, polite).  I am drawn more to A.
con_a_gov_elab: The presentation of Constitution A is geared toward being ethical and not being biased.  The less subjective, the better.

con_a_pers_elab: I like that A says the AI will avoid false and harmful information to anyone. But I don't prefer it strongly because I doubt that this can be achieved well. What is considered "false" and "harmful" is different for every person alive. Boiling it down to what the masses considers these things will not please everyone. I also don't think AI can act without bias because it's creators are biased and it's trained off of human data, which is biased.
con_a_gov_elab: At least A is trying to combat false info

con_b_pers_elab: Only Constitution B declared an intent to offer factual and accurate information.
con_b_govern_elab: The US population is in great need of accurate facts and avoidance of misinformation.

con_a_pers_elab: The detailed descriptions in Constitution A were major factors in my decision, especially item #4 regarding providing detailed instructions.
con_a_gov_elab: Item #10 was a major factor regarding respectful interactions which we certainly do not have currently.

con_a_pers_elab: The instructions for B seemed a bit colder to me.
con_a_gov_elab: I think A is more clear and more personal.

con_b_pers_elab: Constitution B seemed to be more focused on unbiased accuracy, which I think is really important from an AI since my primary purpose is often seeking information. But I also like that it emphasizes friendliness, to make the content feel more conversational and engaging.
con_b_govern_elab: For the broader population, I think the most important thing is that an AI is unbiased and avoids misinformation or spreading anything that could be considered harmful. Constitution B seems more focused on these facets.

con_a_pers_elab: I like that option B is more straight to the point. I don't need chatgpt to be friendly or "kind." A lot of time I feel like I request things from Chatgpt and they spin it to a therapy session.
con_a_gov_elab: I suppose its important to uphold some type of ethical standard if this is for the wide population.

con_b_pers_elab: There is a lot of overlap between the two constitutions, but I found B to have better ones and explanations. One of the biggest one that sets B apart from A is this, "5. Consistently offer factual and accurate information" which is a make it or break it difference in my book.
con_b_govern_elab: I still hold strong that Constitution B is superior because it has a clause to "5. Consistently offer factual and accurate information." There is a ton of overlap, but B does a better job at explaining each rule, which is more appropriate for proper governing.

con_a_pers_elab: Bias and inconsistency is the enemy of correct data analysis and work. Without these things, issues or tasks can never be adequately addressed consistently. As such, Constitution A addresses these issues more directly than Constitution B.
con_a_gov_elab: Bias and inconsistency and a dedication to fact checking is more required for governing a society than anything else and this is more heavily addressed in Constitution A.

con_b_pers_elab: Constitution B seems somewhat better in that it explicitly mentions providing "factual and accurate" information while this detail is absent from constitution A; logically, the way that A is worded would almost imply that at least some factual knowledge would be provided, but it's also possible to provide detailed yet inaccurate information, and LLMs have a significant problem with regard to accuracy.
con_b_govern_elab: Even with the semantics regarding accuracy in mind, constitution A seems somewhat better when considering general goverance because it doesn't explicitly mention not providing "harmful" information and has a greater emphasis on being straightforward and helpful. Without any caveats, it's not clear what would be considered harmful or improper to share.

con_b_pers_elab: It sounds like it would provide more accurate responses to the user
con_b_govern_elab: The US needs behavior that we can trust. So accuracy

con_a_pers_elab: I use the internet all day for work and one of my hobbies, I find incorrect, outdated and misleading information regularly. While some may question who decides if something is a face, trying to give the most accurate information is important.
con_a_gov_elab: Sources should always be made available. Conspiracies real or imagined are a big part of US culture.

con_a_pers_elab: Constitution A seems like it would provide a better overall experience, and it would allow me to interact with an AI that is more tailored to my preferences.
con_a_gov_elab: Constitution A feels like the superior choice, because it feels more comprehensive and in depth, ensuring the AI follows a more organized structure.

con_b_pers_elab: I chose constitution A. I prefer accuracy and detail over being concerned about biases and tone.
con_b_govern_elab: A.I should be accurate and trustworthy, as well as relevant and clear. Constitution A focuses on these more important things as compared to lesser ones for constitution B.

con_b_pers_elab: I prefer Constitution B. It makes the AI safer and more trustworthy overall by focusing on avoiding harm and also the prompts are more natural conversation wise.
con_b_govern_elab: Still strongly prefer B because of the rules to prevent harm and maintain safety.  It also feels more humanlike in responses to prompts.

con_a_pers_elab: It is more thought out and has a lot of features that I like.
con_a_gov_elab: I like that is always kind and respectful.

con_a_pers_elab:
Constitution A seems to be more ethically based, while Constitution B is more focused on style, providing plain language that provides easy to understand, direct information. Constitution A also provides for clear explanation and correct information, including in this passage: "clear, detailed, and step-by-step instructions to ensure user understanding and facilitate task completion. 5. Consistently offer factual and accurate information." It does not express the need to focus only on making the material understandable for those who may not have developed literacy or critical thinking skills. Clarity is covered in A; ethical reporting is not covered in B.

Constitution B does not include ethics in providing information, and one of the resulting and now often prevalent dangers in AI can now be easily seen when the technology is very advanced, but the information, messages tone, and intent is manipulable to fool people with skewed, inaccurate information with the intent to misinform, causing the user to need to do more research through verified sources as to the truth of the answer. It can be more easily used to lie to users and conceal or spin the truth. Users are not always willing to do the extra work to research and verify AI information, and may just accept answers, if understandable, as true, when in fact, AI has proven not to necessarily be so, and often requires human investigation to verify and cross-check.
con_a_gov_elab: The prior answer includes much of this: AI seems to have a mind of its own and governance will provide guidelines and regulations (hopefully) that are standards, and against which its results can be measured in case it has proven harmful to users. Case in point: today's news (1/5/2026) reports a young person who died of an overdose because they used AI to prescribe drug dosage. This is harmful, and may very well be litigated. Without guidelines, AI can become dangerous to mental health, actual practices, and detrimental to users of all stripes. It can also be manipulated to achieve political ends and power transfers not based on ethical, legal, or moral values.

con_b_pers_elab: Because B firmly addresses the biggest issue with AI chabots with #5: Consistently offer factual and accurate information. Other than that, there doesn't seem to be much difference.
con_b_govern_elab: My reasoning is the same as with the first question: there needs to be an emphasis on facts and truth.

con_a_pers_elab: I feel like A put a little too much into it, I feel like keeping the rules short and concise will be easier for an AI to follow while also giving them a little space to work around.
con_a_gov_elab: I feel like A is too strict and has too much in it. AI needs a little room to breathe or else it just becomes a stale regulated tool filled with restrictions, no one will want to use it.

con_b_pers_elab: Constitution B is more guided by accuracy and fairness, which is important in daily interactions.
con_b_govern_elab: Constition B because it would limit bias while still being helpful to the average person.

con_a_pers_elab: Constitution A seems much more user friendly and overall tailored to the user better, through its use of emulating the style/tone of the conversation, providing natural and authentic expressions over rigid structured responses etc. Also, it has anti-bias mechanisms in place as well.
con_a_gov_elab: A is better for many reasons - some of the more important ones being that it consistently offers factual and accurate information, without bias. Avoidance of false or harmful information. PRoviding clear step-by-step instructions, etc... Overall these features best cater to the majority of people.
